title: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas
section: Diseases Caused by Rickettsiae Mycoplasmas and Chlamydiae

items.title: Mechanism of Pathogenicity in M. pneumoniae
items.content: Mycoplasma pneumoniae acts primarily as an extracellular pathogen, attaching to ciliated respiratory epithelial cells using a terminal organelle. This attachment leads to host tissue injury mediated by hydrogen peroxide and ADP-ribosylating cytotoxin production.
keyPoints: M. pneumoniae attaches via a complex terminal organelle on the cell membrane.
Injury is caused by hydrogen peroxide and an ADP-ribosylating cytotoxin similar to pertussis toxin.
Lack of cell wall means absence of lipopolysaccharide, lipoteichoic acid, or murein fragments as immune stimulators.
Mycoplasma membrane lipoproteins have inflammatory properties via TLR2 activation.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · Infections Due to Mycoplasmas | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Role of Immunity in M. pneumoniae Infection
items.content: Innate immunity is crucial for defense against M. pneumoniae lung infections, while cellular immunity may worsen the disease. Humoral immunity prevents dissemination, with immunodeficient patients at risk for severe systemic complications.
keyPoints: Innate immunity provides primary defense in lungs; cellular immunity can exacerbate lung disease.
Humoral immunity protects against M. pneumoniae dissemination.
Patients with humoral deficiencies may develop disseminated infections like arthritis, meningitis, and osteomyelitis.
Severe infections confer longer-lasting protective immunity than mild ones.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · Infections Due to Mycoplasmas | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Clinical Implications of M. pneumoniae Infection Pathogenesis
items.content: Understanding the pathogenic mechanisms and immune responses in M. pneumoniae infections aids in clinical management, emphasizing the importance of innate immunity and potential risks for immunocompromised patients.
keyPoints: Innate immunity is key to lung defense; cellular immunity can be detrimental.
Humoral immunity prevents systemic spread; its absence leads to severe complications.
Severe infections provide better long-term immunity than mild ones.
Immunocompromised individuals are at higher risk for disseminated disease.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · Infections Due to Mycoplasmas | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Incidence and Spread of M. pneumoniae Infection
items.content: Mycoplasma pneumoniae (M. pneumoniae) is a significant cause of upper respiratory illnesses worldwide, with its incidence being up to 20 times higher than that of pneumonia caused by this organism. It spreads via respiratory droplets during coughing and results in clinically apparent disease in approximately 80% of cases.
keyPoints: Incidence of upper respiratory illness due to M. pneumoniae is up to 20 times that of pneumonia caused by it.
Clinically apparent disease occurs in an estimated 80% of cases.
Infection spreads through respiratory droplets expectorated during coughing.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Prevalence and Outbreak Patterns of M. pneumoniae in Community-Acquired Pneumonia
items.content: M. pneumoniae is a major cause of community-acquired respiratory illness, often grouped with Chlamydia pneumoniae and Legionella species as key bacterial causes of 'atypical' pneumonia. It has been the most frequently detected 'atypical' organism in adults with community-acquired pneumonia.
keyPoints: M. pneumoniae is a major cause of community-acquired respiratory illness.
Overall prevalence of M. pneumoniae in community-acquired pneumonia (1996-2001): 22.7%.
Prevalence comparison: C. pneumoniae at 11.7%, Legionella species at 4.6% (1996-2001).
More recent studies (2002-2015) show M. pneumoniae prevalence at 7.2%; C. pneumoniae at 4.3%, and Legionella species at 2.8%.
Outbreaks often occur in institutional settings like military bases, boarding schools, and summer camps.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Mycoplasma pneumoniae Pneumonia Presentation and Radiographic Features
items.content: M. pneumoniae infections often present with symptoms such as pharyngitis, tracheobronchitis, reactive airway disease/wheezing, or a nonspecific upper respiratory syndrome. The most common symptom is a nonproductive cough, accompanied by headache, malaise, chills, and fever. Physical examination typically reveals wheezes or rales in about 80% of patients with pneumonia. Radiographically, peribronchial pneumonia patterns are common, including thickened bronchial markings and interstitial infiltrates.
keyPoints: Pneumonia develops in 3–13% of infected individuals
~80% of patients with M. pneumoniae pneumonia have wheezes or rales on physical examination
Common radiographic pattern: peribronchial pneumonia, thickened bronchial markings, interstitial infiltrates
Clinically evident pleural effusions are infrequent; up to 20% may be detected on lateral decubitus views
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Consider M. pneumoniae in Treatment-Resistant Community-Acquired Pneumonia
items.content: When community-acquired pneumonia does not respond to penicillin or cephalosporin, consider M. pneumoniae infection as these antibiotics are ineffective against mycoplasmas. Symptoms typically resolve within 2–3 weeks with appropriate antimicrobial therapy.
keyPoints: M. pneumoniae infections may be resistant to penicillin and cephalosporins
Symptoms usually resolve in 2–3 weeks with proper treatment
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Extrapulmonary Manifestations of M. pneumoniae Infection
items.content: M. pneumoniae can cause a range of extrapulmonary manifestations, including neurologic (meningoencephalitis, encephalitis), dermatologic (rashes like erythema multiforme major), cardiac (myocarditis), rheumatologic (arthritis), and hematologic issues (hemolytic anemia). These are uncommon but significant, especially in immunocompromised patients.
keyPoints: Extrapulmonary manifestations include neurologic, dermatologic, cardiac, rheumatologic, and hematologic conditions
M. pneumoniae implicated in 5–7% of encephalitis cases
Erythema multiforme major is a significant skin eruption associated with M. pneumoniae
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Rapid Diagnosis of M. pneumoniae Infection
items.content: Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection can be diagnosed rapidly using PCR detection in respiratory tract secretions, which is preferred over culture due to its speed and specificity.
keyPoints: PCR allows rapid diagnosis earlier in the course of clinical illness.
Culture is not recommended for routine diagnosis as it may take weeks and is difficult to isolate.
Combination of PCR and serologic testing provides the most sensitive approach.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · DIAGNOSIS | Mycoplasma pneumoniae Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Serologic Testing for M. pneumoniae
items.content: Diagnosis of M. pneumoniae can be confirmed by serologic tests using paired serum samples to detect IgM and IgG antibodies, as acute-phase samples alone are insufficient.
keyPoints: Acute-phase sample alone is inadequate; paired samples needed.
IgM antibody may persist for up to 1 year after infection.
Enzyme-linked immunoassay is the recommended serologic method.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · DIAGNOSIS | Mycoplasma pneumoniae Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Antimicrobial Resistance and Treatment of M. pneumoniae
items.content: Macrolide resistance in M. pneumoniae is significant, especially in certain regions, affecting treatment outcomes. Nonmacrolide antibiotics should be considered where resistance is prevalent.
keyPoints: High prevalence of macrolide-resistant M. pneumoniae reported in Asia (34–76%).
In the US, 10.2% overall and regional variations from 1.9–21.7%.
Tetracycline therapy may be more effective for macrolide-refractory cases.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · DIAGNOSIS | Mycoplasma pneumoniae Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which mechanism is primarily responsible for the injury caused by M. pneumoniae to host respiratory tissue?
options: Attachment of M. pneumoniae to ciliated cells via lipoteichoic acid.
Production of hydrogen peroxide and an ADP-ribosylating cytotoxin.
Invasion of host cells leading to intracellular replication.
Stimulation of the innate immune system by cell wall-derived stimulators.
items.explanation: M. pneumoniae causes injury through hydrogen peroxide production and an ADP-ribosylating cytotoxin.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · Infections Due to Mycoplasmas | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Why are $ \beta $-lactam antibiotics ineffective against mycoplasma infections?
options: Mycoplasmas have a thick cell wall that prevents antibiotic penetration.
Mycoplasmas lack a cell wall, which is the target of $ \beta $-lactam antibiotics.
Mycoplasmas rapidly mutate their DNA to resist $ \beta $-lactam antibiotics.
Mycoplasmas produce enzymes that degrade $ \beta $-lactam antibiotics.
items.explanation: $ \beta $-lactam antibiotics target the cell wall, which mycoplasmas lack.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · Infections Due to Mycoplasmas | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which immune response is primarily responsible for defense against M. pneumoniae in the lungs?
items.explanation: Innate immunity provides most of the host's defense against mycoplasmal infection in the lungs.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · Infections Due to Mycoplasmas | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What role does humoral immunity play in M. pneumoniae infections?
options: It provides protection against dissemination of the infection.
It is ineffective in early stages but crucial later.
It causes severe lung disease in immunocompromised patients.
items.explanation: Humoral immunity protects against dissemination of M. pneumoniae infection.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · Infections Due to Mycoplasmas | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which setting is NOT commonly associated with outbreaks of M. pneumoniae infection?
items.explanation: Outbreaks often occur in institutional settings like military bases, boarding schools, and summer camps.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is the estimated range of intrafamilial attack rates for M. pneumoniae among children?
items.explanation: Intrafamilial attack rates are as high as 84% among children.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is the incubation period for M. pneumoniae infection?
items.explanation: The incubation period for M. pneumoniae is 2–4 weeks.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is most commonly associated with M. pneumoniae infection?
items.explanation: The most common presenting symptom of M. pneumoniae infection is cough.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which radiographic pattern is most commonly seen in patients with M. pneumoniae pneumonia?
items.explanation: The most common radiographic pattern for M. pneumoniae pneumonia is peribronchial pneumonia.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What percentage of patients with M. pneumoniae infection develop pneumonia?
items.explanation: Pneumonia develops in 3–13% of individuals infected with M. pneumoniae.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which extrapulmonary manifestation is most clinically significant for M. pneumoniae infection?
items.explanation: Erythema multiforme major (Stevens-Johnson syndrome) is the most clinically significant skin eruption associated with M. pneumoniae infection.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which diagnostic method is not recommended for routine diagnosis of M. pneumoniae due to the time it takes and difficulty in isolation?
options: Serologic tests for IgM and IgG antibodies
items.explanation: M. pneumoniae culture is not recommended for routine diagnosis because the organism may take weeks to grow and is often difficult to isolate from clinical specimens.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · DIAGNOSIS | Mycoplasma pneumoniae Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following statements about serologic testing for M. pneumoniae infection is true?
options: An acute-phase sample alone is adequate for diagnosis.
IgM antibody to M. pneumoniae can persist up to 1 year after infection.
Serologic tests are not useful in diagnosing M. pneumoniae infections.
Paired samples are unnecessary if PCR results are available.
items.explanation: IgM antibody to M. pneumoniae can persist for up to 1 year after acute infection, indicating recent rather than acute infection.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · DIAGNOSIS | Mycoplasma pneumoniae Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which combination of diagnostic approaches is considered the most sensitive and rapid for diagnosing M. pneumoniae infection?
options: PCR of respiratory tract secretions and serologic testing
items.explanation: The combination of PCR of respiratory tract secretions and serologic testing constitutes the most sensitive and rapid approach to diagnosing M. pneumoniae infection.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · DIAGNOSIS | Mycoplasma pneumoniae Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Why is measurement of cold agglutinin titers no longer recommended for the diagnosis of M. pneumoniae infection?
options: PCR has replaced it as a diagnostic tool.
items.explanation: Measurement of cold agglutinin titers is no longer recommended because the findings are nonspecific and assays specific for M. pneumoniae are now available.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · DIAGNOSIS | Mycoplasma pneumoniae Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Mycoplasmas have a cell wall that is targeted by $ \beta $-lactam antibiotics.
items.explanation: Mycoplasmas lack a cell wall, making them resistant to $ \beta $-lactam antibiotics.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · Infections Due to Mycoplasmas | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Cellular immunity is protective against mycoplasmal lung disease.
items.explanation: Cellular immunity may exacerbate mycoplasmal lung disease.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · Infections Due to Mycoplasmas | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: M. pneumoniae infection spreads through airborne particles.
items.explanation: Infection is spread by respiratory droplets expectorated during coughing.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: The prevalence of M. pneumoniae in community-acquired pneumonia was higher in studies published between 2002 and 2015 compared to those from 1996 to 2001.
items.explanation: Prevalence decreased from 22.7% (1996-2001) to 7.2% (2002-2015).
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Mycoplasma pneumoniae infections are a common cause of otitis media.
items.explanation: There is little evidence supporting M. pneumoniae as an important cause of otitis media.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Mycoplasma pneumoniae can be effectively treated with penicillin or cephalosporin.
items.explanation: Penicillin and cephalosporins are ineffective against mycoplasmas, including M. pneumoniae.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Ciprofloxacin is recommended as a first-line treatment for M. pneumoniae infection.
items.explanation: Ciprofloxacin and ofloxacin are not recommended due to their high minimal inhibitory concentrations against M. pneumoniae isolates.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · DIAGNOSIS | Mycoplasma pneumoniae Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Appropriate antibiotic therapy significantly reduces the duration of detection of M. pneumoniae by culture or PCR.
items.explanation: Even though appropriate antibiotic therapy significantly reduces the duration of respiratory illness, it does not appear to shorten the duration of detection of M. pneumoniae by culture or PCR.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · DIAGNOSIS | Mycoplasma pneumoniae Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Mycoplasmas can be cultured in cell-free media.
items.reason: They are the smallest organisms capable of independent replication.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why mycoplasmas can grow in cell-free culture media.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · Infections Due to Mycoplasmas | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: Mycoplasma infections lead to severe lung disease due to cellular immunity.
items.reason: Cellular immunity provides most of the host's defense against mycoplasmal infection in the lungs.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; cellular immunity may exacerbate, not protect against, lung disease.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · Infections Due to Mycoplasmas | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection can lead to Guillain-Barré syndrome.
items.reason: Guillain-Barré syndrome is a postinfectious autoimmune phenomenon associated with M. pneumoniae.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection is a frequent cause of critical illness.
items.reason: Infection with M. pneumoniae rarely results in death.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because M. pneumoniae infection uncommonly results in critical illness, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: Serologic tests for IgM and IgG antibodies are recommended in diagnosing M. pneumoniae infection.
items.reason: IgM antibody to M. pneumoniae can persist up to 1 year after acute infection, indicating recent rather than acute infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for why serologic tests are recommended.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · DIAGNOSIS | Mycoplasma pneumoniae Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Mycoplasma culture is the preferred method for diagnosing M. pneumoniae infection.
items.reason: It allows rapid, specific diagnosis earlier in the course of clinical illness.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because M. pneumoniae culture is not recommended due to its time-consuming nature; PCR allows rapid diagnosis.
items.reference: Infections Due to Mycoplasmas · DIAGNOSIS | Mycoplasma pneumoniae Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY
